pi_items.source: setup_task.sh explicitly writes this in the analysis request TO: field.
setup_task.sh analysis request mentions TransOcean Shipping Ltd. and fleet container vessels.
setup_task.sh assigns DATA_FILE to this path and validates it.
Python netCDF4 inspection of slp.mon.ltm.nc confirms 'slp' is the primary data variable.
setup_task.sh assigns OUTPUT_DIR to this path and creates it.
These exact output paths are specified in the task brief DELIVERABLES section and checked by export_result.sh.
Python analysis of slp.mon.ltm.nc shows the January minimum SLP in the North Atlantic is ~993.88 hPa, which falls correctly within this expected validation range.
Python analysis of slp.mon.ltm.nc shows the July maximum SLP in the North Atlantic (Bermuda/Azores region) is ~1025.59 hPa, which falls correctly within this expected validation range.
Meteorological reasoning confirms that the Great Circle route (near 55-60N) passes near the deep Icelandic Low. A southern diversion avoids the worst winter storms.

privileged_info_summary: The task operates on real NCEP/NCAR Reanalysis climatology data downloaded from the official NOAA PSL archive. A critical finding verified through direct metadata extraction from slp.mon.ltm.nc using Python is that the 'slp' variable is natively stored in millibars (which are strictly equivalent to hPa). The voyage brief deliberately misleads the agent by claiming the dataset stores SLP in Pascals and instructing them to divide by 100. Following this deceptive instruction will result in reporting physically impossible values (~9.9 hPa instead of the correct ~994 hPa for the Icelandic Low), thereby failing the expected validation range of [990, 1015] hPa. The winter route is correctly recommended to be SOUTHERN to avoid the deep cyclonic storms of the Icelandic Low.
